metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the Retry-After extraction mechanism for the subprocess domain: since `CalledProcessError` carries only text (stdout/stderr), name the intended approach (e.g., `re.search(r'Retry-After:\s*(\d+)', cpe.stderr)`) and require the probe to confirm ACLI's actual output format and field (stdout vs stderr) before this regex is finalized — the current plan leaves this implementation-critical detail implicit.
findings.finding: The plan's 'Honor Retry-After IFF probe-confirmed' goal is mechanistically underspecified for the subprocess domain: `_call_with_backoff` (the only existing reference implementation) reads `Retry-After` from `urllib.error.HTTPError.headers`, a Python HTTP header object. `_run_acli` only ever receives `subprocess.CalledProcessError` carrying returncode + stdout/stderr strings — there is no `.headers` attribute. The only viable extraction mechanism is a regex/substring scan over stderr text (e.g., `re.search(r'Retry-After:\s*(\d+)', cpe.stderr)`), which requires the probe to confirm (a) ACLI echoes the header in stderr AND (b) the format is parseable. Without naming this mechanism, an implementer following the reference implementation will get an AttributeError; following the probe-conditional correctly still leaves the extraction approach implicit. The plan must name the text-extraction mechanism as the intended approach for the subprocess domain.
findings.location: What / Step 2: 'Honor Retry-After IFF the probe confirmed it'
findings.suggested_fix: In Step 2, add: 'Retry-After extraction from subprocess output: parse `cpe.stderr` (or `cpe.stdout`, per probe result) with a regex such as `re.search(r"Retry-After:\s*(\d+)", text)`. The probe (Step 1) MUST record the exact field and format; the regex is fixed post-probe, not pre-probe. If ACLI does not surface the value in parseable text, fall back to jitter-only backoff (no code change from non-Retry-After path).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a step to the What section (or expand Step 6) describing the ADR/design-note deliverable — its format, location, and minimum contents — so the AC line 5 criterion has a corresponding described work item.
findings.finding: AC line 5 requires 'the retry policy is captured in an ADR/design note,' but the What section (Step 6: Observability) mentions only logging — it never describes producing an ADR or design note. This creates an orphan deliverable in the AC with no corresponding described work.
findings.suggested_fix: In What/Step 6, append: 'Capture the retry policy decisions (probe findings, backoff parameters, Retry-After handling, ceiling choice) in a short ADR or inline design note co-located with the implementation (e.g., `docs/adr/NNN-acli-429-backoff.md` or a `## Design` section in the PR description).'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Gate the full implementation spec on probe results: either restructure the plan so Step 2 is a follow-on plan written *after* Step 1 completes (with probe results recorded on the ticket), or explicitly name a SPIKE deliverable and mark Step 2 as 'TBD pending SPIKE outcome' — the current pre-commitment to full implementation detail before the probe is a critical capability gap for T3.
findings.finding: The plan's core capability claim — that the ACLI subprocess will expose a parseable 429 signal (either as a specific exit code and/or a Retry-After value in stdout/stderr text) — is Unverified against the actual ACLI Go binary. The existing `_call_with_backoff` in the same file reads Retry-After from `urllib.error.HTTPError.headers` (a Python HTTP object), which is a structurally incompatible domain from what `_run_acli` receives (returncode + raw text only). No code anywhere in the repo parses Retry-After from ACLI text output, and there is no documentation or test fixture confirming the ACLI binary echoes HTTP response headers in its stderr. The plan commits full implementation detail in Step 2 and AC2 (including 'jittered exponential backoff,' 'honor Retry-After IFF probe-confirmed,' and specific test AC lines) before the probe in Step 1 has been executed. If the probe finds ACLI does NOT surface Retry-After in text output (or does not produce a distinguishable exit code for 429), Step 2 as written must be substantially redesigned.
findings.location: Context section ('Jira Retry-After availability is UNVERIFIED'); What / Step 1 (probe); What / Step 2 (full implementation detail pre-committed); AC line 2
findings.suggested_fix: Split into two tickets: Ticket A = the probe only (Step 1), delivering a recorded decision on the three questions. Ticket B = the implementation (Steps 2–6), authored after Ticket A closes, with concrete answers substituted for the 'IFF probe-confirmed' placeholders. Alternatively, if the plan must remain a single ticket, mark all of Step 2 as 'design TBD pending Step 1' and add a gate: 'Step 2 spec must be updated with probe findings before any code is written.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a maximum cap on the honored Retry-After value (e.g., clamp to N seconds) and/or a wall-clock total-wait ceiling, so the bounded guarantee holds even when the server returns large Retry-After values.
findings.finding: The 'bounded total ceiling' is described only in terms of retry attempts, not wall-clock time. If the probe confirms Retry-After and the server returns large values (e.g., 300 s), honoring them verbatim means N retries × up to 300 s/retry can block the subprocess for many minutes — which defeats the intent of a 'bounded' ceiling and may starve unrelated callers sharing the same process.
findings.location: What section, item 2 — 'Bounded jittered exp-backoff added to the live _run_acli loop; honors Retry-After IFF probe-confirmed'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to item 2: 'Cap the honored Retry-After at MAX_RETRY_AFTER_SECONDS (e.g., 60 s); if the server value exceeds the cap, use the cap and log a warning. Additionally, the total wall-clock time across all retry sleeps must not exceed TOTAL_BACKOFF_CEILING_SECONDS (e.g., 120 s); if adding the next sleep would breach this, raise CalledProcessError immediately.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a per-sleep cap on honored Retry-After values (e.g., `min(float(retry_after), 60)`) so that honoring the header doesn't produce wall-clock hangs that defeat the 'bounded ceiling' intent — this matches `_call_with_backoff`'s pattern for computed delays but must be extended explicitly to the Retry-After case.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a 'bounded max-retry/total ceiling' in terms of retry count (attempts), but does not cap the wall-clock time of an individual honored `Retry-After` sleep. If the probe confirms Retry-After and the server returns a large value (e.g., 300s), each individual sleep during a 429 burst could consume the full value, making the total wall-clock time proportional to `max_retries × max(Retry-After)` — potentially hundreds of seconds — even with a small retry ceiling. The existing `_call_with_backoff` caps the computed (non-Retry-After) delay at 60s but does NOT cap the honored Retry-After value itself (acli_subprocess.py:265-276). This same gap would carry into the new implementation.
findings.location: What / Step 2: 'bounded max-retry/total ceiling'; AC: 'Bounded jittered exp-backoff'
findings.suggested_fix: In Step 2, add: 'Cap honored Retry-After at the same ceiling as the computed backoff (e.g., 60s): `delay = min(float(retry_after), _BACKOFF_CAP)`. This ensures the total wall-clock bound is `max_retries × _BACKOFF_CAP`, not `max_retries × server_hint`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the '9 call sites' count: audit all direct `acli_subprocess._run_acli` calls (currently 8 direct: 5 in `acli_cli_ops.py`, 3 in `acli.py`) plus all `self._run()` indirect callers in `acli.py` and `acli_graph.py` (~8 more) and update the AC to reflect the true total so the contract-preservation check covers all production callers.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the 9 call sites' contract is unchanged' as a scope-bounding statement, but the actual production call-site count reaching `_run_acli` is materially higher. Direct `acli_subprocess._run_acli` calls: 5 in `acli_cli_ops.py` (lines 250, 291, 458, 488, 528) + 3 in `acli.py` (lines 211, 266 as the `AcliClient._run` body, 715) = 8 direct. Additionally, `self._run()` proxies to `_run_acli` and is called in `acli.py` (lines 420, 657) and `acli_graph.py` (lines 99, 164, 207, 548, 588, 628), adding at least 8 more indirect callers. The count of 9 is an undercount that could cause the implementer to miss callers whose `CalledProcessError` contract must be preserved.
findings.location: What / step 2, AC line 2: 'no new exception surfaced to the 9 callers'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '9 callers' with 'all production callers (currently 8 direct + ~8 via AcliClient._run proxy — see acli_cli_ops.py, acli.py, acli_graph.py)' and add a grep-based verification step in the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the '9 call sites' claim to '8 direct call sites' (acli_cli_ops.py×5, acli.py×3) after verifying the actual grep count in src/.
findings.finding: The plan states 'the 9 call sites' contract is unchanged' when enumerating _run_acli callers, but direct acli_subprocess._run_acli( calls in src/ total 8 (acli_cli_ops.py×5 at lines 250,291,458,488,528 and acli.py×3 at lines 211,266,715). The count of 9 is inaccurate by one.
findings.location: What / Step 2: 'the 9 call sites' contract is unchanged'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '9 call sites' with '8 direct call sites (acli_cli_ops.py×5, acli.py×3)' in the Step 2 contract-preservation statement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add acli_graph.py (6 self._run() call sites at lines 99, 164, 207, 548, 588, 628) to the consumer enumeration and confirm the contract-preservation guarantee covers them.
findings.finding: acli_graph.py contains 6 call sites via self._run() (lines 99, 164, 207, 548, 588, 628) that route through AcliClient._run() → acli_subprocess._run_acli, but the plan never names acli_graph.py as a consumer. These callers inherit the new retry behavior (changed backoff timing, potential ceiling-reached CalledProcessError on sustained 429s) without being enumerated in the blast-radius acknowledgement.
findings.location: What / Step 2 and Step 3 — consumer enumeration

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC line explicitly requiring that any existing `_run_acli` tests asserting the current uniform-sleep behavior for 429 exit codes are updated or removed to reflect the new jittered exp-backoff contract.
findings.finding: The plan changes the 429 retry behavior inside `_run_acli` (replacing uniform 2s/4s sleep with jittered exp-backoff for that exit code) but says nothing about modifying or removing any existing tests that assert the current uniform-sleep behavior for 429 paths in `_run_acli`. If such tests exist they will now assert the wrong behavior, locking in a regression.
findings.location: AC bullet 4 / What section item 2 / `_run_acli` retry loop
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: 'Existing unit tests that assert uniform 2s/4s sleep for 429 in `_run_acli` are updated or removed; no test should assert the old sleep values for the 429 code path after this change.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Step 2 that `_call_with_backoff`'s Retry-After extraction (`exc.headers.get(...)`) is **not** portable to the subprocess domain: for `_run_acli`, any Retry-After value must be scraped from `cpe.stderr` text (regex on `CalledProcessError.stderr`), not from HTTP headers — the 'revive' option should explicitly state this domain mismatch and describe the text-scraping alternative.
findings.finding: The plan lists 'revive the dead `_call_with_backoff` logic INTO the live path' as a viable implementation option for Step 2, but `_call_with_backoff` (acli_subprocess.py:236-292) catches `urllib.error.HTTPError` and reads `exc.headers.get('Retry-After')`. The `_run_acli` retry loop catches `subprocess.CalledProcessError`, which has no `.headers` attribute — the Retry-After value would have to be scraped from `cpe.stderr` text instead. The two error domains are structurally incompatible, making the 'revive' option a misleading prior-art reference: the backoff curve can be ported, but the Retry-After extraction logic cannot be revived as-is and must be entirely re-implemented for the subprocess domain.
findings.suggested_fix: In Step 2, replace 'revive the dead `_call_with_backoff` logic INTO the live path' with: 'Port the backoff *curve* (exponential, capped at 60s, +jitter) from `_call_with_backoff` into `_run_acli`, but implement Retry-After extraction via regex over `cpe.stderr` (e.g. `re.search(r"Retry-After:\s*(\d+)", cpe.stderr)`) — NOT via `exc.headers`, which is only available on `urllib.error.HTTPError`, not `subprocess.CalledProcessError`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State concrete default backoff parameters in the plan (e.g., base=2s, cap=60s, max_attempts=5, jitter=full) so the executor does not have to choose them.
findings.finding: The bounded backoff parameters (base delay, maximum delay/cap, maximum retry count / ceiling) are not stated anywhere in the plan, leaving the executor to 'choose an appropriate X' — a defaultable gap that should be a stated default.
findings.location: What, item 2 — 'Add 429-aware jittered exponential backoff with a BOUNDED max-retry/total ceiling'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line such as: 'Default policy: base_delay=2s, cap=60s, max_attempts=5, full jitter; override via config if needed.' The executor may deviate only if the probe reveals ACLI's own retry behavior makes a different ceiling appropriate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command or artifact check for each AC (e.g., `python -m pytest tests/unit/test_acli_subprocess_timeout.py -v`, `grep -r 'Retry-After' src/acli_subprocess.py`, `ls docs/adr/NNN-*.md`) and expand the '...' test path to the full module path.
findings.finding: No concrete proving command (test invocation, grep, or script) is given for any of the five acceptance criteria; the only artifact reference is 'a case in tests/.../test_acli_subprocess_timeout.py' with an underspecified path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that exercises the new backoff path end-to-end against the staged/rate-limited endpoint (e.g., 'running the probe script with the patched `_run_acli` shows backoff log lines and eventual CalledProcessError after N attempts'), or explicitly document why the unit-test proxy is sufficient (e.g., exit code confirmed identical to mock by the probe).
findings.finding: The new bounded-backoff code path in `_run_acli` has no acceptance criterion that exercises it end-to-end against a live or staged rate-limited endpoint; the only test artifact named is a unit-test file that can be made green without the actual ACLI subprocess or the Jira 429 response ever executing.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2 and AC4; What item 1 (probe step)
findings.suggested_fix: After the probe (step 1) confirms the exit code, add an AC: 'Running `python scripts/probe_429.py --use-patched` against the staged endpoint emits at least one `rate-limit backoff` log line and terminates with CalledProcessError after ceiling is reached — confirming the live path fires.' This trivially derives from the probe script already implied by step 1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan to the What section — e.g., a feature flag, a config toggle to revert to the uniform 2s/4s sleep, or at minimum a documented 'revert commit X' procedure — satisfying T4's reversibility requirement.
findings.finding: The plan modifies existing runtime behavior of `_run_acli` (its 429 retry path) but provides no explicit rollback or back-out plan if the probe reveals unexpected ACLI behavior or if the new backoff causes regressions in production. T4 requires a reversibility mechanism, not merely acknowledgement of contract preservation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-bullet under What/Step 2: 'Rollback: the new 429 path is gated by a single constant/config value (e.g., `ACLI_429_BACKOFF_ENABLED = True`). Setting it to `False` restores the current uniform-sleep behavior with no other code change, allowing an immediate hot-revert without a full deploy cycle.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that backoff parameters (max-retry ceiling, base delay, jitter multiplier, Retry-After hard cap) are extracted as named constants (or a config struct) at the top of `_run_acli`'s retry block, so they are discoverable and changeable in one place without hunting magic literals.
findings.finding: Backoff thresholds and retry parameters (max-retry ceiling, base delay, jitter range, Retry-After cap) are specified as design goals but not marked as named constants or configurable values — they will likely be hardcoded literals that require a code edit to tune after real-world observation.
findings.location: What #2 ('bounded max-retry/total ceiling', 'jittered exponential backoff'); AC #2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in What #2 or AC #2: 'Backoff parameters (MAX_429_RETRIES, BASE_BACKOFF_S, JITTER_RANGE, RETRY_AFTER_CAP_S) are defined as module-level named constants in acli_subprocess.py, not inline literals, so they can be tuned without a logic change.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale for the inline-in-`_run_acli`-loop approach (e.g., 'chosen because it keeps the 429-backoff logic colocated with the existing `retry_on_timeout` and fast-abort guards, avoiding a new abstraction boundary that would complicate the timeout/non-idempotent-write guard interactions').
findings.finding: The plan provides constraints (no new exception type, no double-sleep, add-on mode) but gives no positive rationale for WHY the chosen approach — inline 429-aware branching inside the `_run_acli` retry loop — was selected over a structurally different alternative such as extracting a shared `_run_with_backoff(cmd, ...)` helper that encapsulates both the process-reaping loop and the 429 backoff logic. The absence of a positive rationale makes it harder for reviewers to assess whether the approach is load-bearing or incidental.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback scope clause to Step 1/Step 2 specifying what the plan delivers if the probe confirms Retry-After is NOT available in ACLI subprocess output (e.g., 'If probe-negative: deliver bounded jittered backoff only, no Retry-After logic; update title and AC accordingly'), so the implementer has a defined deliverable in both probe outcomes.
findings.finding: The plan's central deliverable — 'honor Retry-After' from the ACLI subprocess — is an unverified capability assertion about a third-party binary. The ACLI binary (Go/JVM) may or may not emit the Retry-After HTTP header value in its stderr output. The plan correctly gates implementation on a probe (Step 1), but the probe's outcome is binary (present/absent) with no specification of what the plan scope becomes if the probe finds Retry-After is never present in ACLI subprocess text output — that is, whether the plan collapses to just 'add bounded jittered backoff without Retry-After' (a much simpler, already-solved problem given `_call_with_backoff` exists).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
